message:
Add glossary doc, modernize PTY error handling

- Add docs/glossary.md with terminology definitions
- Update README.md with links to glossary and ansi-reference docs
- Use io::Error::other() instead of io::Error::new(Other, ...) in ftui-pty
- Update beads tracking

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

-{"id":"bd-10i","title":"FrankenTUI: Complete Implementation Epic","description":"# FrankenTUI (ftui) - Master Epic

## Vision
FrankenTUI is a deliberately minimal, high-performance terminal UI kernel that fuses the best surviving abstractions from three Rust codebases (rich_rust, charmed_rust, opentui_rust) into one coherent system.

## Core Promise
ftui is designed so you can build a Claude Code / Codex-class agent harness UI without flicker, without cursor corruption, and without sacrificing native scrollback.

## Three-Library Synthesis (conceptual)
- opentui_rust: Cell grid, diff algorithm, alpha blending, scissor/opacity stacks, grapheme pool (cache-optimal 16-byte cells)
- rich_rust: Segment abstraction, markup parser, measurement protocol (Cow<str> for zero-copy)
- charmed_rust: LipGloss styling, CSS-like properties, theme system, Elm architecture (bitflags property tracking)

## Non-Negotiables (Engineering Contract)
1. One writer owns the terminal - serialized output
2. Untrusted bytes never executed - sanitize by default
3. Diffed + buffered UI only - no ad-hoc println
4. Inline-first is real - preserve scrollback
5. Safe by default, unsafe isolated (repo policy: unsafe forbidden)
6. Deterministic by design
7. Correctness continuously verified

## Performance Budgets (v1)
- Present (80x24, 5% changed): p50 < 1.0ms
- Cell comparison: < 1ns
- 16 bytes per cell (4 per cache line)

## Success Criteria (v1 = Done)
(Directly derived from plan Section 0.11.)

- [ ] Inline mode default is stable (streaming logs + UI does not corrupt scrollback/cursor).
- [ ] PTY tests confirm cleanup on normal exit and panic.
- [ ] Presenter/diff correctness validated by terminal-model tests.
- [ ] Unicode width correctness proven by corpus tests (ZWJ/emoji/combining).
- [ ] Style system is deterministic and documented (explicit masks + inheritance).
- [ ] Runtime supports update/view loop + ticks + batch/sequence cmds + deterministic simulator.
- [ ] Harness-essential widgets exist and are tested (viewport/log viewer, status/panel, prompt/text input, progress/spinner).
- [ ] Docs exist: agent harness tutorial, inline vs alt-screen trade-offs, one-writer rule guidance, windows limitations, compatibility matrix.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-31T22:04:22.713361602Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-31T23:24:44.775656345Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"bd-10i.1","title":"Phase -1: Decision Spikes","description":"# Phase -1: Decision Spikes

## Purpose
Before building a big structure, run small spikes that de-risk the hardest unknowns. These are tiny experiments for fast learning.

## Why This Phase Exists
The plan is ambitious and certain decisions could cause multi-week churn if not resolved early. Decision spikes force us to confront reality before committing to architecture.

## Exit Criteria
One ADR (Architecture Decision Record) per spike capturing:
- Context: What problem are we solving?
- Decision: What did we choose?
- Alternatives considered
- Consequences: What are the tradeoffs?
- Test plan: How we'll know it worked

## Key Spikes Required
1. Inline mode spike: Implement both scroll-region and overlay strategies in tiny prototype
2. Presenter validation spike: Build terminal-model harness to validate output
3. Backend spike: Validate raw mode + resize + input reading across platforms

## Relationship to Other Phases
This phase BLOCKS Phase 0 (Contracts + Workspace Skeleton). We must not proceed with architecture until we've validated our assumptions through concrete experiments.

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Spikes are completed with runnable prototypes (even if throwaway) and notes.
- [ ] ADR-001/002/003 are unblocked and written based on spike outcomes.
- [ ] We have a clear go/no-go decision for:
  - inline mode strategy baseline
  - presenter emission baseline
  - backend selection","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-31T22:04:34.363862676Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T18:37:01.689257059Z","closed_at":"2026-02-01T18:37:01.689232683Z","close_reason":"All three decision spikes completed (bd-10i.1.1/1.2/1.3) and ADRs 001/002/003 written","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-10i.1","depends_on_id":"bd-10i","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-31T22:04:34.363862676Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-10i.1.1","title":"Spike: Inline Mode Strategies","description":"# Spike: Inline Mode Strategies

## Objective
Validate a **correctness-first** inline mode strategy for FrankenTUI: stable UI chrome + streaming logs without flicker, cursor drift, or scrollback corruption.

This spike is explicitly about **choosing and proving** the baseline policy before we harden API and implementation.

## Background: Why Inline Mode Is Hard
Inline mode + streaming logs is where most TUIs fail in practice:
- Logs write while UI is mid-draw (multiple writers, interleaving)
- Cursor position gets lost or restored incorrectly
- Full-screen clears destroy scrollback
- tmux/screen and terminal emulators differ in behavior for cursor save/restore, margins, and sync output

This project’s core promise depends on nailing the policy and cleanup invariants early.

## Strategies Under Test

### Strategy A: Scroll-Region Anchoring (DECSTBM / scroll margins)
Use CSI `t;b r` to set top/bottom margins:
- Constrains scrolling to a region (e.g., log region)
- UI region stays pinned while logs scroll within the region

Pros:
- Can reduce redraw work and cursor thrash
- More "native" scrolling behavior when it works

Cons:
- Muxes (tmux/screen) and some terminals may handle margins inconsistently
- Must be carefully reset on exit, panic, and when switching modes

### Strategy B: Overlay Redraw (universal fallback)
Before writing logs:
1. Save cursor position
2. Temporarily move cursor / clear UI region
3. Write logs (so they land in native scrollback)
4. Redraw UI region
5. Restore cursor

Pros:
- Works in the widest set of environments (baseline correctness)

Cons:
- More redraw work
- Requires an explicit “UI region” policy and predictable redraw sequence

### Strategy C: Hybrid (likely default)
- **Overlay redraw** is always available as the correctness baseline
- Scroll-region anchoring is an internal optimization **only** where proven safe
- Public API exposes policy (“inline mode”) not mechanism (“DECSTBM”) to avoid leaking terminal quirks into user code

## Environments / Compatibility Matrix To Validate
Minimum environments for this spike:
- Plain terminal (no mux)
- Kitty
- WezTerm
- Alacritty
- Ghostty
- tmux
- screen (if available)

Also record:
- Whether DEC 2026 synchronized output is supported and affects flicker
- Whether cursor save/restore should use DEC (`ESC7`/`ESC8`) vs ANSI (`CSI s`/`CSI u`) vs emulation

## Instrumentation Requirements
This spike must produce useful evidence, not vibes.

- Structured logs (via `tracing`) for:
  - enter/exit inline mode
  - log-write begin/end
  - UI redraw begin/end
  - cursor save/restore operations
  - any fallback taken (mux detected, feature probe disabled, etc.)
- In PTY tests, capture and store:
  - the raw byte stream written to the terminal
  - parsed “terminal model” invariants (cursor position, mode flags) where possible

## Deliverables
- A tiny prototype (~200 LOC is fine) that demonstrates both A and B, and the selection logic.
- A written decision (ADR-001) that records:
  - decision
  - fallback rules
  - observed incompatibilities
  - required cleanup invariants

## Tests Required
- PTY integration tests that assert:
  - cursor is restored after each frame present in inline mode
  - terminal modes are restored on normal exit and on panic
  - no full-screen clears are used in inline mode unless explicitly configured
- "Sustained output" scenario: continuous log stream + periodic UI redraw + resize events.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Both Strategy A and B can be exercised by a single prototype with an explicit switch.
- [ ] We have a documented default policy (likely Hybrid) with explicit fallback triggers.
- [ ] PTY test demonstrates no cursor drift and correct cleanup on both normal exit and panic.
- [ ] ADR-001 is unblocked with concrete observations (not just theoretical pros/cons).","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-31T22:07:24.429895270Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T07:06:37.854348307Z","closed_at":"2026-02-01T07:06:37.854322419Z","close_reason":"Spike complete: inline_mode.rs + ADR-001 created, all tests pass","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-10i.1.1","depends_on_id":"bd-10i.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-31T22:07:24.429895270Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-10i.1.2","title":"Spike: Presenter Validation with Terminal Model","description":"# Spike: Presenter Validation with Terminal Model

## Objective
Build a minimal **terminal model** (a deliberately small emulator) to validate that a `present()` cycle produces the expected grid and cursor state.

This spike exists to turn presenter correctness from “looks right on my terminal” into something testable, repeatable, and CI-friendly.

## Why This Spike Matters
The Presenter is the inverse-function layer of the whole system:
- incorrect SGR sequencing leaks style across cells
- incorrect cursor moves corrupt subsequent renders or user input location
- incorrectly terminated OSC 8 hyperlinks cause "sticky" links
- missing resets break muxes and downstream output

If Presenter correctness isn’t proven, every later feature becomes suspect.

## Scope: Terminal Model (Not A Full Emulator)
The model only needs to understand the subset of sequences we emit.

It must track:
- a grid of cells (char/grapheme + style)
- cursor position
- current SGR state
- hyperlink state (OSC 8)
- mode flags relevant to correctness (cursor visibility, alt-screen if applicable, sync output nesting)

Out of scope:
- full VT parsing
- scrollback buffer modeling
- alternate character sets
- full mouse/keyboard decoding

## Sequences To Support (v1 model)
Minimum set (aligned to our planned Presenter behavior):
- CUP: `CSI r ; c H`
- relative cursor moves (CUU/CUD/CUF/CUB) if we emit them
- SGR: `CSI ... m` (at least reset + fg/bg + basic attrs)
- EL/ED: `CSI n K` / `CSI n J` (only the variants we emit)
- OSC 8 hyperlinks: start/end
- DEC 2026 synchronized output: begin/end (track nesting but can ignore visual effects)

## Test Strategy

### Deterministic unit tests
- Known buffers → Presenter output → Model apply → exact grid match
- Explicit cases for:
  - style reset boundaries
  - cursor position after present
  - hyperlink open/close correctness

### Property tests
- Generate random buffers (within constraints) and validate:
  - Presenter output is parseable by the model
  - Model grid equals desired buffer
  - No dangling style or hyperlink state after present

### Debuggability requirements
When a property test fails, we need:
- a small minimized failing case (proptest shrink)
- a readable diff of expected vs actual grid
- a dump of emitted escape sequences (escaped and annotated)

## Deliverables
- `TerminalModel` test harness
- A small “Presenter contract” suite that runs in CI and blocks regressions

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] TerminalModel supports the subset of sequences we emit (explicit list above).
- [ ] Unit tests cover representative edge cases (style/link/cursor correctness).
- [ ] Property tests run in CI and have actionable failure output (diff + byte dump).
- [ ] The model is used as a gate for presenter changes (documented in the operational playbook).","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-31T22:07:38.682217640Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T07:11:31.307207071Z","closed_at":"2026-02-01T07:11:31.307187504Z","close_reason":"Completed: TerminalModel with 27 tests (19 unit + 8 property)","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-10i.1.2","depends_on_id":"bd-10i.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-31T22:07:38.682217640Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-10i.11.3","title":"Implement input parser fuzzing","description":"# Implement input parser fuzzing

## Objective
Fuzz the InputParser to ensure it handles all possible byte sequences without panicking or excessive resource usage.

## Why Fuzzing?
- Terminal input is untrusted
- Malformed sequences are common
- DoS via crafted input is possible
- Parser must be robust

## Fuzz Targets

### Target 1: Raw Byte Parsing
fuzz_target\!(|data: &[u8]| {
    let mut parser = InputParser::new();
    let _ = parser.parse(data);
    // Must not panic
    // Must not hang
    // Must not exceed memory limits
});

### Target 2: Structured Fuzzing
// Generate semi-valid sequences
#[derive(Arbitrary)]
enum FuzzSequence {
    Char(u8),
    Escape,
    Csi(Vec<u8>),
    Osc(Vec<u8>),
    Mouse { button: u8, x: u16, y: u16 },
    Paste(Vec<u8>),
}

### Target 3: Long Sequences
// Specifically test DoS limits
fuzz_target\!(|len: u16| {
    let data = vec\![0x1b, b'[']  // CSI
        .into_iter()
        .chain(std::iter::repeat(b'0').take(len as usize))
        .chain(std::iter::once(b'm'))
        .collect::<Vec<_>>();

    let mut parser = InputParser::new();
    let _ = parser.parse(&data);
    // Should not allocate unbounded memory
});

## Properties to Verify
1. No panics on any input
2. Memory bounded (no OOM from crafted input)
3. Time bounded (no quadratic behavior)
4. Always returns to Ground state eventually
5. DoS limits enforced

## Corpus Seeds
- Valid key sequences
- Valid mouse sequences
- Bracketed paste boundaries
- OSC 8 links
- Malformed UTF-8
- Truncated sequences

## Running Fuzzer
cargo +nightly fuzz run input_parser -- -max_len=4096

## Integration with CI
- Run fuzzer for limited time on each PR
- Maintain corpus in repo
- Report new crashes immediately

## Tests Required
- [ ] Fuzz harness builds
- [ ] Initial corpus covers major cases
- [ ] No crashes in 1-hour fuzz session
- [ ] Memory limits enforced

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Fuzzer integrated in CI
- [ ] No panics found
- [ ] Corpus maintained
- [ ] Coverage tracked","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-31T22:15:47.811194117Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-31T22:22:45.486723842Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-10i.11.3","depends_on_id":"bd-10i.11","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-31T22:15:47.811194117Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-10i.11.3","depends_on_id":"bd-10i.5.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-31T22:22:45.486693585Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-10i.12","title":"Docs: Playbook, Glossary, References","description":"# Docs: Playbook, Glossary, References

## Purpose
Encode the *non-code* parts of PLAN_TO_CREATE_FRANKENTUI__OPUS.md into durable, discoverable docs so future work does **not** require rereading the plan document.

This epic is about:
- capturing the project's intent, constraints, and invariants
- preventing churn (ADRs + playbook)
- making the library *pleasant to adopt* (tutorials + examples)

## Scope (v1)
- Operational Playbook (Chapter 17)
- Terminal Compatibility Matrix (Chapter 16)
- Glossary (Appendix B)
- Risk Register (Appendix C)
- ANSI Reference extraction (Appendix A)
- Migration map / "what we kept" from the three libraries (Chapter 14 + 1.5)
- Public API docs + examples (Section 0.8)

## Non-goals
- Duplicating ADRs here (ADRs remain in bd-10i.10)
- Writing long-form tutorials before the kernel is stable

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Every doc deliverable in PLAN_TO_CREATE_FRANKENTUI__OPUS.md (Chapter 16/17 + Appendices A/B/C + Chapter 14) has a corresponding issue with acceptance criteria and test hooks where applicable.
- [ ] Docs are written with enough context that a contributor can implement features without reopening the original plan.
- [ ] Docs include explicit "undefined behavior" and "out of scope" notes where the plan is opinionated (one-writer rule, inline mode, sanitize-by-default).","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:07:35.746628357Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-31T23:14:45.174558275Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-10i.12","depends_on_id":"bd-10i","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:07:35.746628357Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-10i.12","depends_on_id":"bd-10i.10","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:14:45.174515294Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-10i.12.1","title":"Write Operational Playbook (Chapter 17)","description":"# Write Operational Playbook (Chapter 17)

## Background
Chapter 17 exists to prevent the common failure mode of kernel projects: infinite refinement without a stable deliverable.

The playbook is part technical, part process:
- which PRs require which test gates
- how ADRs are written and frozen
- the recommended shipping order (avoid the widget trap)
- what "agent-ergonomic" means in concrete UX terms

## Deliverable
Create a durable doc (e.g. `docs/operational-playbook.md`) that captures:

### Merge-gate rules (from 17.1)
- Any change touching: inline mode cursor policy, presenter output sequencing, input parser, width measurement
  must include appropriate tests (unit/property/pty/snapshot) and must cite which invariants are preserved.

### ADR discipline (from 17.2)
- ADR template (Context / Decision / Alternatives / Consequences / Test Plan)
- Rules for when an ADR is required

### Minimal deliverables order (from 17.3)
1) Kernel (buffer/diff/presenter + terminal session + inline stability)
2) Runtime (update/view + simulator + snapshot)
3) Harness (reference app proving inline/log/UI)
4) Widgets (only harness essentials)
5) Extras (feature-gated)

### Agent-ergonomic checklist (from 17.4)
- Hello world harness in <200 LOC
- Tool output streaming is one function call
- Pinned bottom UI is one config option
- Temporary full-screen modal supported

### Immediate Next Steps checklist (from 17.5)
Convert the listed "first PRs" into checkboxes and link them to the relevant Beads tasks.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Doc exists with the sections above.
- [ ] Doc explicitly ties each rule to a reason (cursor safety, one-writer, determinism).
- [ ] Doc links to the relevant ADR issues and test-suite epics.
- [ ] Doc includes an "Out of scope" section to prevent accidental widget creep.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"docs","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:07:50.447594010Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T18:43:15.094260140Z","closed_at":"2026-02-01T18:43:15.094232978Z","close_reason":"Created docs/operational-playbook.md with all required sections: merge-gate rules, ADR discipline, deliverables order, agent-ergonomic checklist, immediate next steps, and out-of-scope section. ## Background
The plan includes a short glossary of terms that recur throughout the kernel design. This needs to be in-repo so future contributors don't need the plan doc to understand terminology.

## Deliverable
Create `docs/glossary.md` containing (at minimum) the glossary table below, plus any additional terms introduced by implementation.

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Cell** | Single grid position (content + fg + bg + attrs) |
| **Buffer** | 2D array of Cells representing display state |
| **Frame** | Buffer + metadata for a render pass |
| **Diff** | Set of (x, y) positions that changed between buffers |
| **Presenter** | State-tracked ANSI emitter |
| **Grapheme** | User-perceived character (may be multiple codepoints) |
| **GraphemePool** | Interned storage for complex grapheme clusters |
| **LinkRegistry** | URL storage for OSC 8 hyperlinks |
| **Scissor** | Clipping rectangle for rendering |
| **SGR** | Select Graphic Rendition (style codes) |
| **OSC** | Operating System Command (escape sequence) |
| **CSI** | Control Sequence Introducer (`\x1b[`) |
| **DEC** | Digital Equipment Corporation (terminal standard) |
| **ZWJ** | Zero Width Joiner (connects graphemes into compound) |
| **Porter-Duff** | Compositing algebra for alpha blending |

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `docs/glossary.md` exists.
- [ ] Table is present and correct.
- [ ] Any additional ftui-specific terms (e.g., TerminalWriter, ScreenMode, UiAnchor) are added with concise definitions.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"docs","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:08:02.312315920Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T18:47:56.819651763Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-10i.12.2","depends_on_id":"bd-10i.12","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-31T23:08:02.312315920Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"bd-10i.12.3","title":"Write Risk Register + Mitigation Links (Appendix C)","description":"# Write Risk Register + Mitigation Links (Appendix C)

## Background
The plan includes a small Risk Register to keep engineering effort focused on the failure modes that destroy trust in a terminal UI kernel (cursor corruption, escape injection, width bugs, capability mismatches, unsafe creep, byte bloat, interleaved writes).

We want this risk list in-repo so:
- mitigations stay attached to code and tests

 {"id":"bd-3m7u","title":"Headless Rendering Mode for CI Testing","description":"# Headless Rendering Mode for CI Testing

## Background & Motivation

The plan relies heavily on PTY tests for terminal verification (bd-10i.11.2). However, PTY tests are:
- Slow (process spawning overhead)
- Platform-dependent (different PTY behavior)
- Hard to run in some CI environments (Docker, restricted sandboxes)

**The solution:**
- Headless mode renders to in-memory buffer
- No terminal required - works anywhere
- Enables fast snapshot testing in CI
- Complements (not replaces) PTY tests

**Relationship to existing beads:**
- Extends bd-10i.11.1 (terminal-model test harness)
- Enhances bd-2u4 (snapshot/golden testing)

## Technical Design

```rust
pub struct HeadlessTerm {
    screen: Buffer,
    cursor: (u16, u16),
    captured_output: Vec<u8>,
    style_state: StyleState,
    capabilities: TerminalCapabilities,
}

impl HeadlessTerm {
    pub fn new(width: u16, height: u16) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn process(&mut self, output: &[u8]) { ... }
    pub fn screen_text(&self) -> Vec<String> { ... }
    pub fn assert_matches(&self, expected: &[&str]) { ... }
    pub fn export(&self, path: &Path) -> io::Result<()> { ... }
}
```

## Logging Requirements

All parse errors logged at WARN level with sequence bytes and position. Snapshot mismatches logged at DEBUG with line numbers and diff context.

## E2E Tests

```bash
cargo test --features headless -- --test-threads=1
REGENERATE_GOLDENS=1 cargo test --features headless
./scripts/ci-headless-test.sh --report
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] HeadlessTerm type implemented
- [ ] ANSI parsing matches real terminal behavior
- [ ] Snapshot comparison works with clear diff output
- [ ] CI tests run without PTY
- [ ] Integration with existing test framework
- [ ] All ANSI sequences from plan supported
- [ ] Export capability for debugging","notes":"## ANSI Sequences to Support

The headless terminal must support these sequences used by FrankenTUI:

### CSI (Control Sequence Introducer)
- Cursor movement: CUP (H), CUU (A), CUD (B), CUF (C), CUB (D)
- Erase: ED (J), EL (K)
- Scroll: SU, SD
- SGR (Select Graphic Rendition): colors, bold, italic, underline
- Private modes: ?25h/l (cursor visibility), ?1049h/l (alt screen)

### SGR Attributes
- Reset: 0
- Bold: 1, Dim: 2, Italic: 3, Underline: 4
- Foreground: 30-37, 90-97, 38;5;N, 38;2;R;G;B
- Background: 40-47, 100-107, 48;5;N, 48;2;R;G;B

### OSC (Operating System Command)
- OSC 8 (hyperlinks): parse but don't render as link
- OSC 0 (window title): capture but don't use

### Unsupported (should not crash)
- OSC 52 (clipboard)
- DCS/APC sequences
- Unknown CSI sequences

```rust
impl HeadlessTerm {
    fn process_csi(&mut self, params: &[u16], intermediate: &[u8], final_byte: u8) {
        match final_byte {
            b'H' => self.cursor_position(params),
            b'J' => self.erase_display(params),
            b'K' => self.erase_line(params),
            b'm' => self.sgr(params),
            b'A'..=b'D' => self.cursor_move(final_byte, params),
            _ => {
                tracing::warn!(
                    final_byte = final_byte as char,
                    params = ?params,
                    "Unknown CSI sequence"
                );
            }
        }
    }
}
```

-{"id":"bd-3mo","title":"Implement markup parsing for styled text","description":"PHASE 4/EXTRAS - MARKUP PARSER (Feature-gated)

## Background
Markup parsing allows writing styled text with inline syntax like [bold]text[/bold] or Rich-style markup. Feature-gated as it's not needed by all apps.

## Technical Requirements

### Markup Syntax Options

#### Option A: BBCode-style
[bold]Bold text[/bold]
[italic]Italic[/italic]
[fg=red]Red text[/fg]
[bg=#00ff00]Green background[/bg]
[link=https://example.com]Click here[/link]

#### Option B: Rich-style (Python Rich compatible)
[bold]Bold[/bold]
[red]Red text[/red]
[on blue]Blue background[/on blue]
[link=url]Link text[/link]
[#ff0000]Hex color[/#ff0000]

### Parser Structure

pub struct MarkupParser {
    style_stack: Vec<Style>,
    current_style: Style,
}

impl MarkupParser {
    /// Parse markup string into styled Text
    pub fn parse(&mut self, input: &str) -> Result<Text, MarkupError> {
        let mut spans = Vec::new();
        let mut current_text = String::new();
        
        for token in self.tokenize(input)? {
            match token {
                Token::Text(s) => current_text.push_str(&s),
                Token::StyleStart(style) => {
                    if !current_text.is_empty() {
                        spans.push(Span::styled(
                            std::mem::take(&mut current_text),
                            self.current_style.clone(),
                        ));
                    }
                    self.style_stack.push(self.current_style.clone());
                    self.current_style = self.current_style.merge(style);
                }
                Token::StyleEnd => {
                    if !current_text.is_empty() {
                        spans.push(Span::styled(
                            std::mem::take(&mut current_text),
                            self.current_style.clone(),
                        ));
                    }
                    self.current_style = self.style_stack.pop().unwrap_or_default();
                }
            }
        }
        
        Ok(Text::from_spans(spans))
    }
}

### Error Handling
pub enum MarkupError {
    UnmatchedTag { tag: String, position: usize },
    InvalidColor { value: String },
    InvalidAttribute { name: String },
    NestedLinkNotAllowed,
}

### Extension: Emoji Shortcodes
Optional: :smile: -> 😀, :rocket: -> 🚀

pub fn expand_emoji(input: &str) -> Cow<str> {
    // Fast path: no colons
    if !input.contains(':') {
        return Cow::Borrowed(input);
    }
    // Replace :name: patterns with Unicode
}

### Integration
// Direct usage
let text = markup!("[bold]Hello[/bold] [red]world[/red]");

// Via Text
let text = Text::from_markup("[bold]Hello[/bold]");

// In widgets
Paragraph::new(markup!("[italic]Styled[/italic] text"))

## Tests Required
- [ ] Bold tag parsing
- [ ] Color name parsing
- [ ] Hex color parsing
- [ ] Nested tags
- [ ] Unclosed tag error
- [ ] Escape sequences (\\[)
- [ ] Empty tags
- [ ] Link with URL
- [ ] Performance: 1000 char markup < 100µs

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] BBCode-style syntax supported
- [ ] All style attributes (fg, bg, bold, italic, underline)
- [ ] Link parsing with URL extraction
- [ ] Error handling with positions
- [ ] Escape sequence support
- [ ] Optional emoji expansion
- [ ] Feature gate: markup

 {"id":"bd-3t87","title":"Progressive Rendering for Large Content with Virtualization","description":"# Progressive Rendering for Large Content with Virtualization

## Background & Motivation

Agent harnesses accumulate massive amounts of data over long sessions:
- Tool outputs can be thousands of lines
- LLM responses stream continuously
- Log files grow unbounded

Naively rendering all content causes O(n) render time, making the UI unusable.

**Why this matters:**
- Agent sessions can run for hours, accumulating 100K+ log lines
- Users expect smooth scrolling regardless of content size
- Memory usage must be bounded
- Render time must be O(visible) not O(total)

**Relationship to existing beads:**
- Enhances bd-29v (Viewport/LogViewer) with virtualization
- Works with bd-1iqn (Render Budget) for graceful degradation

## Technical Design

### Core Types

```rust
/// Virtualized content container
pub struct Virtualized<T> {
    /// All items (or reference to external storage)
    items: VirtualizedStorage<T>,
    /// Current scroll offset (in items)
    scroll_offset: usize,
    /// Number of visible items (computed from area height)
    visible_count: usize,
    /// Overscan: extra items rendered above/below visible
    overscan: usize,
    /// Height per item (fixed or measured)
    item_height: ItemHeight,
    /// Scroll velocity for momentum
    scroll_velocity: f32,
}

pub enum VirtualizedStorage<T> {
    /// Owned vec of items
    Owned(Vec<T>),
    /// Reference to external storage with len
    External { len: usize, fetch: Box<dyn Fn(Range<usize>) -> Vec<T>> },
}

pub enum ItemHeight {
    /// All items have fixed height
    Fixed(u16),
    /// Items have variable height, measured lazily
    Variable(HeightCache),
}

/// LRU cache for measured item heights
pub struct HeightCache {
    cache: LruCache<usize, u16>,
    default_height: u16,
}
```

### Virtualized List Widget

```rust
impl<T: RenderItem> Widget for VirtualizedList<T> {
    fn render(&self, area: Rect, buf: &mut Buffer) {
        // Calculate visible range
        let (start, end) = self.visible_range(area.height);
        
        // Apply overscan
        let render_start = start.saturating_sub(self.overscan);
        let render_end = (end + self.overscan).min(self.len());
        
        // Render only visible items
        let mut y = area.y;
        for idx in render_start..render_end {
            if let Some(item) = self.get(idx) {
                let item_height = self.item_height(idx);
                let item_area = Rect::new(area.x, y, area.width, item_height);
                
                if item_area.bottom() > area.y && item_area.top() < area.bottom() {
                    item.render(item_area, buf);
                }
                
                y += item_height;
            }
        }
        
        // Render scrollbar
        if self.len() > self.visible_count {
            self.render_scrollbar(area, buf);
        }
    }
}

impl<T> VirtualizedList<T> {
    fn visible_range(&self, height: u16) -> (usize, usize) {
        let start = self.scroll_offset;
        let visible = match &self.item_height {
            ItemHeight::Fixed(h) => (height / h) as usize,
            ItemHeight::Variable(cache) => {
                // Sum heights until we exceed viewport
                let mut count = 0;
                let mut total_height = 0u16;
                while total_height < height && start + count < self.len() {
                    total_height += cache.get_or_default(start + count);
                    count += 1;
                }
                count
            }
        };
        (start, start + visible)
    }
}
```

### Scroll Handling

```rust
impl<T> VirtualizedList<T> {
    /// Scroll by delta (positive = down)
    pub fn scroll(&mut self, delta: i32) {
        let new_offset = (self.scroll_offset as i32 + delta)
            .max(0)
            .min(self.len().saturating_sub(1) as i32);
        self.scroll_offset = new_offset as usize;
    }
    
    /// Scroll to specific item
    pub fn scroll_to(&mut self, idx: usize) {
        self.scroll_offset = idx.min(self.len().saturating_sub(1));
    }
    
    /// Scroll to bottom (follow mode for logs)
    pub fn scroll_to_bottom(&mut self) {
        if self.len() > self.visible_count {
            self.scroll_offset = self.len() - self.visible_count;
        }
    }
    
    /// Enable follow mode (auto-scroll on new items)
    pub fn set_follow(&mut self, follow: bool) {
        self.follow_mode = follow;
    }
    
    /// Called when items are added
    pub fn on_items_added(&mut self, count: usize) {
        if self.follow_mode {
            self.scroll_to_bottom();
        }
```

```rust
impl<T> VirtualizedList<T> {
    /// Apply scroll velocity (for smooth momentum)
    pub fn tick(&mut self, dt: Duration) {
        if self.scroll_velocity.abs() > 0.1 {
            let delta = (self.scroll_velocity * dt.as_secs_f32()) as i32;
            self.scroll(delta);
            
            // Apply friction
            self.scroll_velocity *= 0.95;
        } else {
            self.scroll_velocity = 0.0;
        }
    }
    
    /// Start momentum scroll
    pub fn fling(&mut self, velocity: f32) {
        self.scroll_velocity = velocity;
    }
}
```

### Memory-Efficient Log Storage

```rust
/// Log storage with bounded memory and optional persistence
pub struct LogRing {
    /// Circular buffer of log lines
    ring: VecDeque<LogLine>,
    /// Maximum lines in memory
    capacity: usize,
    /// Total lines ever received (for accurate scroll positions)
    total_count: usize,
    /// Optional overflow file for persistence
    overflow: Option<OverflowFile>,
}

impl LogRing {
    pub fn push(&mut self, line: LogLine) {
        self.total_count += 1;
        
        if self.ring.len() >= self.capacity {
            if let Some(overflow) = &mut self.overflow {
                // Write evicted line to overflow file
                overflow.write(self.ring.pop_front().unwrap());
            } else {
                self.ring.pop_front();
            }
        }
        
        self.ring.push_back(line);
    }
    
    pub fn get(&self, idx: usize) -> Option<&LogLine> {
        // Map absolute index to ring index
        let ring_start = self.total_count.saturating_sub(self.ring.len());
        if idx >= ring_start && idx < self.total_count {
            self.ring.get(idx - ring_start)
        } else if let Some(overflow) = &self.overflow {
            // Read from overflow (may involve I/O)
            overflow.read(idx)
        } else {
            None
        }
    }
}
```

 {"id":"bd-3tfu","title":"Implement RenderBudget struct with time tracking","description":"# Implement RenderBudget struct with time tracking

## Background
Part of Render Budget Enforcement (bd-1iqn). The RenderBudget struct tracks elapsed time during frame rendering and manages degradation level.

## Technical Design

```rust
use std::time::{Duration, Instant};

/// Render time budget with graceful degradation
pub struct RenderBudget {
    /// Total time budget for this frame
    total: Duration,
    /// When this frame started rendering
    start: Instant,
    /// Current degradation level
    degradation: DegradationLevel,
    /// Per-phase budgets (optional breakdown)
    phases: PhaseBudgets,
}

/// Budget allocation per render phase
pub struct PhaseBudgets {
    /// Budget for widget tree traversal
    pub traverse: Duration,
    /// Budget for diff computation
    pub diff: Duration,
    /// Budget for ANSI emission
    pub present: Duration,
}

impl RenderBudget {
    /// Create a budget with the given total time
    pub fn new(total: Duration) -> Self {
        Self {
            total,
            start: Instant::now(),
            degradation: DegradationLevel::Full,
            phases: PhaseBudgets::default(),
        }
    }
    
    /// Time remaining in this frame budget
    #[inline]
    pub fn remaining(&self) -> Duration {
        self.total.saturating_sub(self.start.elapsed())
    }
    
    /// Check if we should degrade before an expensive operation
    /// estimated_cost: rough estimate of how long the operation takes
    #[inline]
    pub fn should_degrade(&self, estimated_cost: Duration) -> bool {
        self.remaining() < estimated_cost
    }
    
    /// Advance to next degradation level
    pub fn degrade(&mut self) {
        let old = self.degradation;
        self.degradation = self.degradation.degrade();
        tracing::warn!(
            from = ?old,
            to = ?self.degradation,
            elapsed_ms = self.start.elapsed().as_millis(),
            "Degrading render fidelity"
        );
    }
    
    /// Get current degradation level for widgets to check
    #[inline]
    pub fn degradation(&self) -> DegradationLevel {
        self.degradation
    }
    
    /// Check if budget is exhausted (should skip frame)
    #[inline]
    pub fn exhausted(&self) -> bool {
        self.remaining().is_zero() || self.degradation.skip_frame()
    }
    
    /// Record phase entry for detailed timing
    pub fn enter_phase(&mut self, phase: RenderPhase) {
        // For tracing spans
    }
    
    /// Get elapsed time since frame start
    #[inline]
    pub fn elapsed(&self) -> Duration {
        self.start.elapsed()
    }
}

impl Default for PhaseBudgets {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            traverse: Duration::from_millis(4),
            diff: Duration::from_millis(2),
            present: Duration::from_millis(4),
        }
    }
}
```

## Why These Defaults
- Total 16ms = ~60fps feel
- 4ms traverse: widget tree can be complex
- 2ms diff: typically fast with good cell comparison
- 4ms present: includes IO overhead
- Remaining ~6ms: buffer for variance

## Test Requirements
- [ ] remaining() decreases as time passes
- [ ] should_degrade() returns true when cost > remaining
- [ ] exhausted() when remaining is zero OR degradation is SkipFrame
- [ ] degrade() logs warning via tracing

+# Glossary
+
+This glossary defines terms used throughout FrankenTUI's codebase and documentation.
+
+---
+
+## Core Types
+
+| Term | Definition |
+|------|------------|
+| **Cell** | Single grid position containing content (char or grapheme ID), foreground color, background color, and style attributes. 16 bytes. |
+| **CellContent** | 4-byte discriminated union: either a Unicode scalar value (char) or a GraphemeId reference. |
+| **CellAttrs** | Style flags (u16) + link ID (u16) packed into 4 bytes. |
+| **Buffer** | 2D array of Cells representing display state. Row-major storage for cache efficiency. |
+| **Frame** | Buffer + cursor position + hit grid for a render pass. Represents the desired terminal state. |
+| **Diff** | Set of (x, y) positions that changed between front and back buffers. |
+| **ChangeRun** | Contiguous horizontal span of changed cells, used for output optimization. |
+
+---
+
+## Rendering
+
+| Term | Definition |
+|------|------------|
+| **Presenter** | State-tracked ANSI emitter. Tracks current SGR state, link state, and cursor position to minimize output. |
+| **TerminalModel** | Minimal VT emulator used for deterministic testing. Parses ANSI sequences and updates internal state. |
+| **Front Buffer** | The last-presented buffer (what the terminal currently shows). |
+| **Back Buffer** | The buffer being rendered to (the desired next state). |
+| **Scissor** | Clipping rectangle for rendering. Push/pop stack with monotonically decreasing intersection. |
+| **Opacity Stack** | Compositing stack where each layer multiplies alpha. Product stays in [0.0, 1.0]. |
+
+---
+
+## Text & Typography
+
+| Term | Definition |
+|------|------------|
+| **Grapheme** | User-perceived character. May be multiple Unicode codepoints (e.g., emoji with skin tone). |
+| **GraphemeId** | 32-bit reference to an interned grapheme. Encodes pool slot (24 bits) + display width (7 bits). |
+| **GraphemePool** | Reference-counted interned storage for complex grapheme clusters that don't fit in 4 bytes. |
+| **Segment** | Atomic unit of styled text with `Cow<str>` storage and optional style. |
+| **Span** | Higher-level wrapper around text + style. Builder for creating styled text units. |
+| **Line** | Collection of spans representing a single line of text. |
+| **Text** | Multi-line styled text. Collection of Lines with layout information. |
+| **Width Cache** | LRU cache for text display width measurements. Default 1000 entries. |
+
+---
+
+## Terminal
+
+| Term | Definition |
+|------|------------|
+| **TerminalSession** | RAII guard that owns terminal state. Ensures cleanup on exit and panic. |
+| **TerminalWriter** | Single gate for all terminal output. Enforces one-writer rule. |
+| **TerminalCapabilities** | Detected terminal features: color support, mux detection, synchronized output, etc. |
+| **ScreenMode** | Display mode: `Inline` (preserves scrollback) or `AltScreen` (full-screen). |
+| **UiAnchor** | Where to pin UI in inline mode: `Top` or `Bottom`. |
+| **InlineStrategy** | Inline mode rendering approach: `OverlayRedraw`, `ScrollRegion`, or `Hybrid`. |
+| **Raw Mode** | Terminal mode where input is unbuffered and special keys are passed through. |
+
+---
+
+## Escape Sequences
+
+| Term | Definition |
+|------|------------|
+| **ANSI** | American National Standards Institute. Terminal control standard. |
+| **CSI** | Control Sequence Introducer (`ESC [` or `\x1b[`). Starts most control sequences. |
+| **SGR** | Select Graphic Rendition. CSI codes for setting text style (bold, color, etc.). |
+| **OSC** | Operating System Command (`ESC ]`). Used for setting title, hyperlinks, clipboard. |
+| **DCS** | Device Control String. Less common, used for some terminal features. |
+| **APC** | Application Program Command. Rarely used in modern terminals. |
+| **DEC** | Digital Equipment Corporation. Maker of VT terminals. Some sequences use DEC-specific codes. |
+| **OSC 8** | Hyperlink protocol. `ESC ] 8 ; ; URL ST` to start, `ESC ] 8 ; ; ST` to end. |
+| **DEC 2026** | Synchronized output mode. Batches terminal updates to reduce flicker. |
+
+---
+
+## Layout
+
+| Term | Definition |
+|------|------------|
+| **Rect** | Axis-aligned rectangle with x, y, width, height. 0-indexed. |
+| **Sides** | Padding/margin values for top, right, bottom, left. CSS-like tuple conversions. |
+| **Measurement** | Size hints with min/max constraints for layout negotiation. |
+| **Constraint** | Layout sizing rule: Fixed, Percentage, Min, Max, or Ratio. |
+| **Flex** | Flexbox-like layout container with direction, gap, and constraints. |
+
+---
+
+## Colors
+
+| Term | Definition |
+|------|------------|
+| **PackedRgba** | 4-byte packed color with RGB + alpha channel. Supports Porter-Duff compositing. |
+| **ColorProfile** | Terminal color capability: `Mono`, `Ansi16`, `Ansi256`, or `TrueColor`. |
+| **Color Downgrade** | Pipeline to convert colors to lower capability: TrueColor → 256 → 16 → Mono. |
+| **StyleFlags** | 16-bit bitmask for text attributes (bold, italic, underline, etc.). |
+
+---
+
+## Runtime
+
+| Term | Definition |
+|------|------------|
+| **Program** | Elm-like runtime: Model + update + view loop. |
+| **Model** | Application state. Owned by the runtime. |
+| **Cmd** | Side effect to execute. Returned by update functions. |
+| **Subscription** | Polling-based event source (ticks, input, etc.). |
+| **RenderBudget** | Per-frame byte/time budget for output. Controls graceful degradation. |
+
+---
+
+## Unicode
+
+| Term | Definition |
+|------|------------|
+| **ZWJ** | Zero Width Joiner (U+200D). Connects graphemes into compound characters (e.g., family emoji). |
+| **Combining Character** | Character that modifies the preceding base character (accents, diacritics). |
+| **Display Width** | Number of terminal columns a character occupies. ASCII = 1, CJK = 2, some emoji = 2. |
+| **WTF-8** | Encoding that extends UTF-8 to handle unpaired surrogates. Used in some width testing. |
+
+---
+
+## Compositing
+
+| Term | Definition |
+|------|------------|
+| **Porter-Duff** | Compositing algebra for alpha blending. Defines how source and destination combine. |
+| **Alpha Blending** | Combining colors based on transparency values. |
+| **Source Over** | Porter-Duff operation where source is drawn over destination. Most common mode. |
+
+---
+
+## Testing
+
+| Term | Definition |
+|------|------------|
+| **PTY** | Pseudo-terminal. Used for integration tests that spawn real terminal processes. |
+| **Snapshot Test** | Test that compares output against saved "golden" file. |
+| **Property Test** | Test that verifies invariants across random inputs (proptest). |
+| **Terminal Model Test** | Test that validates presenter output by parsing it with TerminalModel. |
+
+---
+
+## Terminal Multiplexers
+
+| Term | Definition |
+|------|------------|
+| **Mux** | Terminal multiplexer (tmux, screen, zellij). Adds a layer between application and terminal. |
+| **tmux** | Popular terminal multiplexer. Detected via `TMUX` environment variable. |
+| **screen** | GNU Screen multiplexer. Detected via `STY` environment variable. |
+| **zellij** | Modern terminal multiplexer. Detected via `ZELLIJ` environment variable. |
+
+---
+
+## Safety Concepts
+
+| Term | Definition |
+|------|------------|
+| **One-Writer Rule** | Only one entity may write to the terminal at a time. Prevents cursor corruption. |
+| **Sanitization** | Stripping control sequences from untrusted output. Enabled by default. |
+| **Raw Passthrough** | Allowing ANSI sequences through without sanitization. Opt-in, dangerous for untrusted content. |
+| **RAII** | Resource Acquisition Is Initialization. Pattern ensuring cleanup via Drop trait. |
+
+---
+
+## Project-Specific
+
+| Term | Definition |
+|------|------------|
+| **ftui** | FrankenTUI. This project. |
+| **Kernel** | Core rendering infrastructure (ftui-render, ftui-core). Must be stable before widgets. |
+| **Harness** | Agent harness. Reference application demonstrating inline mode + log streaming. |
+| **LogSink** | API for routing in-process logs through FrankenTUI. Implements `std::io::Write`. |
+| **PtyCapture** | API for capturing subprocess output via PTY. Preferred for tool output. |
+
